results.title: A Survey of Efficient Reasoning for Large Reasoning Models: Language, Multimodality, and Beyond
results.content:
Current research primarily focuses on long CoT reasoning in o1/R1-like models, while efficient RL methods for alternative reasoning structures, such as parallel search, tree of thoughts, or graph of thoughts, remain largely unexplored. [...] yeo2025demystifyinglongchainofthoughtreasoning analyzes RL design choices for reasoning, revealing that extremely long CoT reasoning (approaching context limits) paradoxically reduces accuracy.
Their proposed cosine reward function provides intuitive guidance—gradually increasing rewards for meaningful reasoning steps while penalizing excessive length.
They also identify “length hacking”, where models artificially extend reasoning on difficult questions through repetition rather than genuine problem-solving, highlighting the challenge of aligning length-based rewards with actual reasoning quality. [...] ARM wu2025arm further generalizes reasoning mode switching by treating different reasoning formats (e.g., direct answer, short CoT, long CoT, and code-based reasoning) as explicit actions, and applies reinforcement learning to select formats that balance correctness and reasoning cost.

Incentivizing Dual Process Thinking cheng2025incentivizingdualprocess explicitly distinguishes fast (System 1) and slow (System 2) reasoning processes, and introduces process-aware incentives that encourage models to invoke long-form reasoning only when necessary.

### 5.5 Alternative Efficiency Signals

results.title: BABILong: Testing the Limits of LLMs with Long Context Reasoning ...
results.content: only 10-20% of the context and their performance declines sharply with increased reasoning complexity. Among alternatives to in-context reasoning, Retrieval-Augmented Generation methods achieve a modest 60% accuracy on single-fact question answering, independent of context length. Among context extension methods, the highest performance is demonstrated by recurrent memory transformers after fine-tuning, enabling the processing of lengths up to 50 million tokens. The BABILong benchmark is extendable to any length to support the evaluation of new upcoming models with increased capabilities, and we provide splits up to 10 million token lengths.

results.title: Demystifying Reasoning Models - by Cameron R. Wolfe, Ph.D.
results.content:
Explore many alternative solutions.

For some concrete examples of these reasoning trajectories, see this blog post. Notably, the long CoT used by OpenAI’s reasoning models are “internal”, meaning that they are hidden from the user when interacting with the model. Instead, the user sees a model-written summary of the long CoT; see below.

The long CoT output of reasoning models gives us an easy way to control the inference-time compute of an LLM. If we want to spend more compute on solving a problem, we can simply generate a longer CoT. Similarly, less complex problems can be solved with a shorter CoT, thus saving compute at inference time. [...] Long CoT. The main difference between a reasoning model and a standard LLM is the ability to “think” before answering a question. The reasoning model’s thoughts are just long chains of thought—or long CoT for short, sometimes referred to as a reasoning trace or trajectory—outputted by the LLM. This long CoT is generated no differently than any other sequence of text. However, these reasoning trajectories exhibit very interesting properties that are more akin to search algorithms than vanilla text generation. For example, the model will:

Think through each part of a complex problem.

Decompose complex problems into smaller, solvable parts.

Critique its own (partial) solutions and find errors.

Explore many alternative solutions. [...] Long CoT (and inference-time scaling). The key distinction between reasoning models and standard LLMs is their output structure. Instead of just directly generating a final answer (with an optional concise explanation), reasoning models generate a long CoT that describes their reasoning process in great detail. This long CoT can be of variable length, enabling controllable compute costs at inference time: longer CoT = more tokens = more compute. In this way, using more compute at inference time—by generating a longer CoT—has become a tool that can allow users to dynamically improve a model’s reasoning capabilities.

results.title: LLM and Prolog: the logical alternative to chain-of-thought reasoning
results.content: Prolog, with its robust logic programming and deterministic reasoning, fills this gap by offering precise, rule-based solutions that enforce logical consistency and enable transparent reasoning paths. Similarly, Python-based reasoning leverages LLMs’ ability to generate and execute numerical computations, making it highly effective for financial analysis, revenue calculations, and other quantitative tasks. [...] improvements of leased properties are amortized over the shorter of the life of the applicable lease term or the estimated useful life of the asset . impairment of long-lived assets when indicators of impairment are present, the company evaluates the carrying value of long-lived assets, other than goodwill, in relation to the operating performance and future cash flows or the appraised values of the underlying assets. the company may adjust the net book value of the underlying assets based upon such cash flow analysis compared to the book value and may also consid- er appraised values . assets to be disposed of are adjusted to the fair value less than the cost to sell if less than the book value. the company recorded impairment charges of approximately $ 0.5 million and $ 0.6 million in [...] Furthermore, by leveraging the powerful pattern recognition capabilities of LLMs to extract logical predicates, neural symbolic reasoning significantly reduces the burden of manually crafting rules, making it more scalable and adaptable to a wider range of problem domains.

results.title: Demystifying Long Chain-of-Thought Reasoning in LLMs
results.content: some of the alternative ways to look inside the black box so we ended last time talking about how traditional ways of looking at these llms might not be enough like just counting the length of the Chain of Thought or looking for specific keywords right those simpler metrics just don't give us the complete picture it's not quite that straightforward yeah makes sense so what are some of these I guess alternative ways of measuring what's going on what do the researchers suggest well one interesting one they talk about is using KL Divergence KL Divergence hm okay that sounds pretty technical what is that exactly it's uh well imagine you have a jares and at the start they're all red that represents the lm's initial knowledge right everything it knows before we start okay I'm following so far [...] was getting better at solving the problems but it wasn't necessarily using more of these thinking keywords exactly and that's a bit of a mystery it suggests that maybe the llm is learning in a more subtle way that there's something deeper going on beneath the surface okay so it seems like we're getting closer to understanding lm's reason but there's still a big piece of the puzzle missing right and the researchers realized that maybe these traditional metrics like just looking at the length of the Chain of Thought or counting keywords maybe that's not enough so we need a new way to measure and understand what's happening inside these llms right and luckily that's exactly what we're going to explore in the next part of our Deep dive some of the alternative ways to look inside the black box
